S5_challenge.title: S5 → Challenging Project
bullets: ⚠️ LEAD WITH THE CHALLENGE STATEMENT — not 3 minutes of background

[SITUATION] At Scotiabank, AML feature engineering is a major bottleneck — translating FINTRAC regulatory documents into executable detection features takes days of manual work by experts who understand both compliance and data schemas.
My senior manager Phume had published a research paper proposing a multi-agent LLM architecture to solve this, but there was no working implementation.
I took the initiative to build the full system from scratch as an independent research project, on top of my regular responsibilities.

[TASK — THE CHALLENGE] The biggest challenge was making LLMs produce production-quality code reliably — getting consistent, validated output from a non-deterministic system. That's fundamentally hard because every time you call the model, you can get a slightly different answer.
[ACTION] To solve this, I designed and built the entire platform end-to-end: a FastAPI backend with 6 specialized AI agents, each following a Perceive-Reason-Act loop.

The pipeline reads regulatory text, adapts to the target database schema, generates Python feature code, validates it through deterministic checks, runs Isolation Forest anomaly detection, and verifies each alert against the same regulatory text — closing the loop with full traceability.

The key to solving the reliability challenge was the self-correction loop: when the Validator catches errors — missing columns, type mismatches, empty outputs — it feeds structured feedback back to the Engineer agent for automatic retry, up to 5 iterations. This is what makes the system reliable despite LLMs being non-deterministic.
⚠️ SAY 'self-correction loop with structured feedback' — NOT 'reinforcement learning'
I also built a React frontend so the team can monitor each agent working in real time.
[RESULT] The pipeline compiles regulatory text into executable features in 2-3 minutes versus days manually.
It achieved AUC of 90-91.5% on the IBM AML benchmark.
We presented the full research to the SVP and director, and we're presenting to the full research team next week.
⚠️ STOP HERE

S5_compliance.title: S5 → Regulated Environment / Compliance
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, a core challenge in AML is ensuring every detection feature can be traced back to its regulatory source — FINTRAC regulatory documents. The current manual process ensures this because experts read the regulation and hand-code features. But it takes days per update.

[TASK — THE COMPLIANCE CONSTRAINT] I built a multi-agent LLM pipeline to automate this, but the key design constraint was: every generated feature must maintain full traceability back to the original regulatory text. In a regulated banking environment, you can't have a black box generating features with no audit trail.

[ACTION] I built traceability directly into the architecture. Agent 1 reads the FINTRAC regulatory text. Agent 2 maps it to the target database schema. Agent 3 generates Python feature code. Agent 4 validates through deterministic checks. Agent 5 runs Isolation Forest anomaly detection. And critically, Agent 6 verifies every single alert against the original regulatory text — closing the loop.
The self-correction mechanism also serves a compliance purpose: when the Validator catches errors, it creates a structured log of what went wrong and how it was fixed, so every output is auditable.
⚠️ SAY 'self-correction loop with structured feedback' — NOT 'reinforcement learning'
I also built a React frontend so compliance teams and data scientists can monitor exactly what each agent is doing and trace any feature back to its regulatory source.
[RESULT] The pipeline achieved AUC of 90-91.5% while maintaining full regulatory traceability — something that's non-negotiable in AML.
We presented to the SVP and director, and the traceability design is one of the reasons leadership is confident enough to explore putting this into production.
⚠️ STOP HERE

S6_teamwork.title: S6 → Teamwork / Leadership / Collaboration
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, our team of three was tasked with building a Customer Risk Score — a framework that scores customers 0 to 100 based on transactional behavior, to complement the existing static Client Risk Rating.

[TASK — THE TEAM DYNAMIC] All three of us independently proposed feature engineering approaches. My senior manager selected mine, so I took the lead on feature engineering — but I still needed buy-in from my teammates who had invested time in their own proposals.

[ACTION] Rather than just overruling them, I focused on showing why my approach solved a problem theirs didn't. My teammates proposed rolling window features — counting transactions in 30, 60, 90 day windows. The issue is rolling windows produce multiple rows per customer, but we need strictly one score per customer. I introduced Discrete Wavelet Transform instead.

I explained it in simple terms: 'What we really want is two things — the customer's overall trend and their short-term frequency. DWT does both in one step and gives us one score per customer. No merging needed.'

Once I showed a side-by-side comparison on a sample, they were convinced. From there, we worked collaboratively — I led the feature engineering, and we collectively validated the features using multiple models.
We prototyped in Pandas, then translated to PySpark together once cluster access was granted — millions of rows across six FINTRAC channels.
[RESULT] The feature set passed validation across both supervised and unsupervised models.
The CRS framework is now the foundation for Sprint 2, and the features, model validation, and data pipeline all feed into the production system.
⚠️ STOP — don't go longer

S6_conflict.title: S6 → Conflict / Disagreement / Persuasion
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, our team of three was building a Customer Risk Score — scoring customers 0 to 100 based on transactional behavior across millions of transactions and six FINTRAC channels.
All three of us independently proposed feature engineering approaches. My senior manager selected mine and I took the lead — but each teammate had invested time in their own approach.

[TASK — THE DISAGREEMENT] The biggest challenge was a technical disagreement over feature engineering. My teammates proposed rolling window features — for example, counting transactions in the past 30, 60, 90 days for each customer.

[ACTION — THE ARGUMENT] I identified a fundamental flaw: when you compute rolling windows, you get multiple rows per customer at different time points. But our final CRS score needs to be strictly one number per customer. Merging rolling windows back to customer-level requires choosing which window to keep, or averaging them — and either way you lose information or introduce inconsistency.

[HOW I PERSUADED THEM] Rather than just saying 'my way is better,' I explained it simply: 'What we really want is to capture two things — the customer's overall trend and their short-term frequency. Discrete Wavelet Transform does both in one step and outputs a single score per customer. No need to merge multiple time windows.'
Then I showed a side-by-side comparison on a sample dataset — my approach versus rolling windows. The results spoke for themselves.
Once they saw the evidence, they were fully convinced. We moved forward together.
We prototyped in Pandas, then translated to PySpark to handle the full scale.
[RESULT] The feature set passed validation across both supervised and unsupervised models.
The CRS framework is now the foundation for Sprint 2.
⚠️ STOP — don't go longer

S6_scaling.title: S6 → Large Data / Scaling
bullets: [SITUATION] At Scotiabank, our team was building a Customer Risk Score — scoring customers based on transactional behavior. The dataset was massive: millions of transaction records spanning six FINTRAC channels — electronic funds transfers, large cash transactions, casino disbursements, and more.

[TASK — THE SCALE CHALLENGE] The challenge was twofold: first, engineer features that work at customer-level granularity across this volume of data, and second, build a pipeline that could actually process it within reasonable time.
[ACTION] I led the feature engineering using Discrete Wavelet Transform, which captures both overall trends and short-term frequency in a single pass per customer — no expensive window joins needed.

We started prototyping in Pandas on sample data to validate the approach quickly. But once we needed to run against the full dataset — millions of rows across all six channels — Pandas couldn't handle it.

When cluster access was granted, I translated the entire pipeline from Pandas to PySpark. This required rethinking some operations — Pandas relies on row-level indexing while PySpark is columnar and distributed — but the core logic transferred cleanly because I'd designed the feature engineering to be aggregation-friendly from the start.
We validated features across both supervised and unsupervised models on the full dataset.
[RESULT] The feature set passed validation at full scale.
The CRS framework — features, model validation, and data pipeline — is now the foundation for Sprint 2 and feeds into the production system.
⚠️ STOP

S1_deadline.title: S1 → Quick Decision / Tight Deadline
bullets: [SITUATION] At the Government of Canada, my team managed a major tax credit program with a workflow that had been manual for decades. Each Tax Credit Officer used their own unstandardized methods, creating inconsistency and a significant bottleneck.

[TASK — THE TIME PRESSURE] I was tasked with leading a full process re-engineering — designing and deploying a fully automated detection system. My manager's directive was to maximize accuracy to build trust and ensure adoption.

[ACTION — THE QUICK DECISION] I analyzed over 7,000 historical case files and quickly realized the decision-making process was too complex and non-linear for simple models. I had to make a fast call: the government traditionally relies on logistic regression for interpretability, but the data demanded something more powerful.

Given the business priority was predictive power over interpretability — and these officers had been making decisions for 20 years with no written rules, just intuition — I chose XGBoost. A tree-based model that mimics the way humans actually make decisions: focus on the most distinguishing signal first.
Once performance was proven, I collaborated with the back-end engineering team to integrate it into production.
The entire project — from initial analysis to production deployment — was completed in 2.5 weeks.
[RESULT] 99.7% accuracy, automated 98% of manual reviews, over $700,000 in annual cost savings.
The system is still deployed and running in production at the Government of Canada today.
⚠️ STOP HERE — don't add extras after $700K

S1_problemsolving.title: S1 → Problem-Solving / Analytical Thinking
bullets: [SITUATION] At the Government of Canada, my team managed a major tax credit program where case reviews had been done manually for decades — each officer using their own unstandardized methods.
[TASK] I was tasked with designing and deploying a fully automated detection system. My manager's primary directive was to maximize accuracy.
[ACTION — THE DIAGNOSTIC PROCESS] I started by analyzing over 7,000 historical case files to understand the patterns in how decisions were actually made.

What I found was that the decision-making process was highly complex and non-linear — officers weren't following a simple set of rules. They were using experience and intuition built over decades, weighing many factors in context-dependent ways.

This told me logistic regression would be insufficient — it assumes linear relationships and weights every feature independently. The data needed a model that could capture non-linear interactions and prioritize the most important signals automatically.

I chose XGBoost specifically because tree-based models handle exactly this: they find the most discriminating feature first, then split further — much closer to how the officers were actually making decisions.
I validated the model, then collaborated with the back-end engineering team to integrate it into production. Analysis to deployment in 2.5 weeks.
[RESULT] 99.7% accuracy, automated 98% of manual reviews, over $700,000 in annual cost savings.
⚠️ STOP HERE

S1B.title: S1-B: CAVCO — Stakeholder Communication (SAME PROJECT, DIFFERENT MOMENT)

[ACTION] I used an analogy: 'Imagine you're trying to tell a cat from a dog.'

'But a tree-based model asks one question first: does it bark? That single question gets you 90% of the way there.'
'That's actually how our brains work — we don't analyze every detail when making a quick decision, we spot the most obvious signal first and decide from there.'

(⭐ BUSINESS CONNECTION) 'These officers have been doing case reviews for 20 years with no written rules or guidelines — just experience and intuition. We needed a model that mimics that human decision-making process, focusing on the most distinguishing signals first.'
[RESULT] They got it. We moved forward with XGBoost.
The system is still deployed in production at the Government of Canada today.

intro_why_rbc.title: Why RBC / Why this role?
bullets: Three reasons.

First, the JD overlaps tightly with what I already do — Python, SQL, PySpark, machine learning on customer-level financial data in a regulated environment. The methodology transfers; the domain shift is from AML risk to credit risk.

S5_followups.title: S5 S2A — Technical Follow-Up Q&A
bullets: (Q: Why 6 agents instead of one LLM?)

Each agent is specialized for one step — Regulatory Analyst reads the document, Schema Adapter maps to our data, Feature Engineer writes Python code, Deterministic Validator checks syntax and types, Isolation Forest runs detection, RCC Verifier checks alerts against original regulation.
Separated because each step has different failure modes. I can pinpoint exactly where the pipeline fails and retry only that step, not the whole thing.
(Q: Explain the reinforcement learning component.)
⚠️ CAREFUL — correct the framing

To clarify — the current implementation uses a structured self-correction loop, not true reinforcement learning. When the validator catches errors, it sends structured feedback back to the engineer agent for retry. That is deterministic feedback, not RL.

The reinforcement learning component is Phase 2 — once we have enough logged iterations from the team using the platform, we will have the data to train a reward model that optimizes generation quality over time. We are in the data collection stage for that now.
(Q: Only 1.5% AUC improvement — why is that significant?)
The baseline model already had 90% AUC with hundreds of manually engineered features built over years.
A 1.5 point improvement from a single auto-generated feature shows the platform can discover signal that human experts missed.
The real value is not one feature — it is that the platform can generate and test hundreds of features in minutes instead of days. We are scaling it across multiple regulatory scenarios now.
(Q: What would you change if you rebuilt it?)
Three things. First, stronger statistical tests in the validator beyond IV and KS — my director flagged this.
Second, build a feedback storage layer from day one so we collect data for the RL phase faster — that was an afterthought in v1.
Third, add automated A/B comparison between agent-generated and manually engineered features so we can continuously benchmark quality without human review.
(Q: What metrics did you use for feature quality?)
Three checks in the validator. First, AST safety — make sure the LLM did not import dangerous libraries like OS that could override or delete data.
Second, column alignment — verify the generated code only references columns that actually exist in our data schema.

Third, predictive power — KS statistic and Information Value. If IV falls below our threshold, the self-correction loop triggers and sends the work back to the engineer or analyst agent to regenerate.

S6_followups.title: S6 CRS — Technical Follow-Up Q&A
bullets: (Q: What is the difference between CRR and CRS?)

CRR is the existing system — classifies customers based on static KYC attributes at account opening. Age, profession, location, home address. But it does not capture what the customer is actually doing.

CRS is what I am building — scores customers 0-100 based on transactional behavior. A customer making transactions at 3 AM or suddenly receiving money from 10 different people in one day — CRR misses that, CRS catches it.
(Q: Why PySpark instead of Pandas?)
Millions of rows across six FINTRAC transaction channels. Does not fit in memory on a single machine. PySpark distributes the processing across a cluster.
I prototyped in Pandas first, then translated to PySpark for production scale.
(Q: What is DWT and why did you choose it over rolling windows?)
Discrete Wavelet Transform. It decomposes a time series into both high-frequency components (sudden spikes in activity) and low-frequency components (gradual behavioral trends).
Rolling windows can capture trends but they collapse customer-level granularity — you lose the individual signal. DWT preserves both.
My senior manager Phume evaluated three approaches from the team and selected mine.
(Q: What were the strongest features?)
Network features had the highest impact — fan-in and fan-out patterns. Fan-in means many accounts sending money to one account. Fan-out means one account distributing to many.
These patterns directly map to money laundering typologies.
We had six feature dimensions total: amount, temporal, channel, geographic, network, and currency.
(Q: How did the team dynamic work?)
Team of three. I led feature engineering and proposed the DWT approach.
Phume evaluated all three of our approaches and selected mine.
We divided the six feature dimensions across the team, but I owned the methodology and the Pandas-to-PySpark translation.

PSI: fix bin edges from baseline, don't re-bin independently

themes.label: Lasso vs Ridge vs LARS (L1 vs L2)
Train/test parity ≠ no leakage (4 failure modes)
SAS — if vs where, merge, nodup

tech_lr_exec.title: LR — Explain to an Executive
bullets: It's a classification algorithm for when the target is categorical — like 'will this customer default yes or no'.
The main objective is to learn the relationship between features and the probability of the outcome.
Analogy: we're predicting whether a student passes or fails based on hours studied. The model outputs a probability between 0 and 1.
Under the hood it models log-odds as a linear function of features; the sigmoid maps that to a probability.
Trained by maximizing log-likelihood — equivalent to minimizing cross-entropy.
In credit: the output IS the PD; coefficients translate to odds ratios via exp(β) — business-intuitive reading of each feature's effect.

tech_lr_significance.title: LR — Two features both p=0, which is more significant?
bullets: Four angles to compare — don't just look at p-value.
1. Coefficient magnitude — larger |β| means stronger effect. Exponentiate to get odds ratio — business-intuitive.
2. Standard error — smaller SE means more precise estimate (even if both p-values saturate at 0).
3. VIF — check for multicollinearity. VIF > 10 suggests the two are correlated and inflating each other's apparent significance.
4. Single-variable fit — fit the model with each variable alone, compare residual deviance. Lower deviance = that variable carries more signal.
⭐ Say: 'p-value is necessary but not sufficient for ranking features — I'd look at coefficient × SE × VIF × single-variable fit together.'

tech_lr_assumptions.title: LR — Assumptions + Diagnostics
bullets: 1. Linearity in the logit — log-odds is a linear function of features.
2. Independence of observations.
3. No perfect multicollinearity — check VIF (>10 problematic).
4. Large sample size — MLE is asymptotic.
5. No influential outliers — check Cook's distance, standardized residuals.
Unlike linear regression, LR does NOT assume normality of features, constant variance of errors, or linear relationship between features and target.
Diagnostics: Hosmer-Lemeshow for calibration, ROC/AUC for discrimination, deviance residuals for fit.

tech_linreg_assumptions.title: Linear Regression — Assumptions
bullets: Model: Y = Xβ + e where e ~ MVN(0, σ²).
1. Mean of errors is 0.
2. Constant variance of errors (homoscedasticity).
3. Errors are IID (independent, identically distributed).
4. Errors are normally distributed.
5. Linearity — relationship between X and Y is linear.
6. No multicollinearity among predictors.
Diagnostics: residual plots (homoscedasticity), QQ plot (normality), VIF (multicollinearity), Durbin-Watson (autocorrelation).

tech_multicollinearity.title: Multicollinearity — Detect + Solve
bullets: What it is: two or more predictors are linearly related. Distorts coefficient estimates and p-values, but doesn't hurt prediction.
DETECT — method 1: Correlation matrix. Pairwise |r| > 0.7 is a warning sign.
DETECT — method 2: VIF = 1/(1 - R²_j) where R²_j is the R-squared from regressing feature j on all others.
VIF thresholds: 1 orthogonal | <5 OK | 5-10 concern | >10 serious.
⚠️ statsmodels gotcha: variance_inflation_factor does NOT add constant automatically — must add_constant() first or VIFs are inflated.
SOLVE — 1. Engineer a ratio (e.g., loan/income → DTI) — preferred in credit.
SOLVE — 2. Drop the less predictive variable (lower IV).
SOLVE — 3. Regularization — L2 Ridge tolerates multicollinearity, L1 Lasso auto-selects.
SOLVE — 4. PCA — destroys interpretability, rarely usable in regulated credit.

tech_bias_variance.title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
bullets: Bias = (average estimated value) − (true value). Low bias = model fits the training data well.
Variance = variability of estimates across different training samples. Low variance = stable across data.
Tradeoff: simple models → high bias, low variance (underfit). Complex models → low bias, high variance (overfit).
Ideal: low bias + low variance — hard in practice, usually a tradeoff.
Adding predictors / complexity decreases bias and increases variance.
In credit validation: over-complex PD models may fit training beautifully but fail on out-of-time test — classic high-variance symptom.

tech_regularization.title: Lasso vs Ridge vs LARS (L1 vs L2)
bullets: Both add a penalty to linear regression loss to handle multicollinearity and prevent overfitting.
RIDGE (L2): penalty = λ Σβ². Shrinks coefficients toward 0 but never exactly zero. Good when all features matter somewhat.
LASSO (L1): penalty = λ Σ|β|. Can shrink coefficients to exactly zero → automatic variable selection. Sparse solutions.
LARS (Least Angle Regression): opposite approach — start with all weights zero, add variables with most explanatory power.
ELASTIC NET: mix of L1 + L2 (α controls balance).
As λ → 0: reduces to standard regression. As λ → large: penalty dominates, coefficients → 0.
In credit: regulators usually prefer explicit variable selection over Lasso — but Lasso is great for exploratory benchmarking.

tech_variable_selection.title: AIC / BIC / p-value — Variable Selection
bullets: AIC = 2k − 2 ln(L). k = number of parameters, L = likelihood. LOWER is better. Penalizes complexity lightly.
BIC = k ln(n) − 2 ln(L). Penalizes complexity MORE than AIC (penalty grows with sample size). Prefers simpler models.
Rule of thumb: AIC for prediction, BIC for inference / simpler models.
P-value: individual significance of each coefficient (H0: β=0). Common threshold ≤ 0.05.
Stepwise selection: forward (add one at a time), backward (remove one at a time), bidirectional.
In credit risk: use stepwise + IV threshold (typically >0.1) + business judgment. Rarely only one criterion.
LASSO can auto-select but regulators often want explicit, documented variable selection rationale.

tech_ab_testing.title: A/B Testing + Hypothesis Testing
bullets: Split sample into control (A, original) and treatment (B, new version).
Statistical test compares means or proportions — t-test (continuous), chi-square (categorical), z-test (proportions, large n).
H0: no difference between groups. H1: there is a difference.
P-value < α (usually 0.05) → reject H0 → significant effect.
Watch for: sample size (power), multiple testing (Bonferroni), peeking (sequential testing adjustment).
In model validation: A/B compares champion vs challenger model.

tech_bayes.title: Bayes' Theorem
bullets: P(A|B) = P(B|A) · P(A) / P(B)
Reads: probability of A given B equals probability of B given A times prior on A, divided by marginal on B.
Classic example: base rates matter. If a test is 99% accurate but the disease is rare (1 in 10,000), most positives are false positives.
In credit: posterior PD = prior PD × likelihood of observed behavior / marginal.
Applied in: Bayesian LR (weak prior to fix quasi-separation), Naive Bayes classifier, Bayesian backtesting.

tech_monte_carlo.title: Monte Carlo Simulation
bullets: A computational algorithm that uses repeated random sampling to estimate the distribution of outcomes in complex systems.
Example: simulate 10,000 future stock price paths using volatility + interest rate assumptions. Average gives expected price; spread gives risk.
In credit / risk: used in economic capital modeling (CreditMetrics, CreditRisk+). Simulate portfolio losses to compute VaR or expected shortfall.
More simulations → more accurate, but more compute time.
Accuracy depends on input quality (GIGO) and number of runs.
Validator angle: review the simulation's random seed control, convergence diagnostics, and assumption sensitivity.

tech_el_formula.title: EL = PD × LGD × EAD (MEMORIZE)
bullets: EXPECTED LOSS = Probability of Default × Loss Given Default × Exposure at Default.
PD: probability the borrower defaults over a horizon (usually 12 months for Basel).
LGD: % of exposure that's lost if they default (after recovery). Typically 20-70% for unsecured; lower for secured.
EAD: dollar amount at risk at time of default. For term loans = outstanding balance. For revolving = drawn + CCF × undrawn.
Regulatory capital is a function of EL AND unexpected loss (UL) — UL drives capital requirements via Basel formulas.
In validation: each parameter is independently validated. PD model, LGD model, EAD model are separate validation exercises.

tech_pd_quarterly.title: PD Quarterly → Annual (MEMORIZE)
bullets: Given 4 consecutive quarterly conditional PDs (CPDQ1, CPDQ2, CPDQ3, CPDQ4), annual absolute PD:
PD_1y = 1 − (1 − CPDQ1)(1 − CPDQ2)(1 − CPDQ3)(1 − CPDQ4)
Intuition: 1 minus the probability of surviving ALL four quarters.
Expanded: CPDQ1 + (1−CPDQ1)·CPDQ2 + (1−CPDQ1)(1−CPDQ2)·CPDQ3 + (1−CPDQ1)(1−CPDQ2)(1−CPDQ3)·CPDQ4
⭐ This was on the 学姐 interview. If asked, WRITE this down clearly, don't fumble.
Same pattern generalizes to any time aggregation using conditional survival probabilities.

tech_woe_iv.title: WoE + IV (Weight of Evidence + Information Value)
bullets: Given a binned feature, for each bin i: P_g(i) = goods in bin i / total goods; P_b(i) = bads in bin i / total bads.
WoE_i = ln(P_g(i) / P_b(i)) — log-ratio of good to bad density.
IV = Σ_i (P_g(i) − P_b(i)) · WoE_i — sums across bins.
⭐ IV = symmetrized KL divergence between good and bad distributions — principled info-theoretic interpretation.
Thresholds: <0.02 useless | 0.02-0.1 weak | 0.1-0.3 medium | 0.3-0.5 strong | >0.5 SUSPICIOUS (leakage).
Laplace smoothing (ε=0.5) prevents log(0) in sparse bins.
WoE transform makes non-linear features linear for LR — standard in credit scorecard development.

tech_auc_ks_gini.title: AUC / KS / Gini — Discrimination Metrics
bullets: AUC = P(score(bad) > score(good)). Range [0.5, 1]. In credit: 0.75-0.85 good, >0.90 SUSPICIOUS (leakage).
KS = max_t |F_good(t) − F_bad(t)| — maximum vertical distance between cumulative distributions. In credit: 0.40-0.60 good.
GINI = 2·AUC − 1. In credit: 0.50-0.70 good. Heavily used in credit (sometimes preferred to AUC).
Always report BOTH train and test. Gap = overfitting diagnostic (>5pp investigate, >10pp real overfit).
⚠️ Both very high on train AND test can mean LEAKAGE — matching high doesn't rule out contamination.

tech_psi.title: PSI — Population Stability Index
bullets: Formula: PSI = Σ_i (P_a(i) − P_e(i)) · ln(P_a(i) / P_e(i)) — where P_e = expected (baseline), P_a = actual (current).
Thresholds: <0.10 stable | 0.10-0.25 monitor | >0.25 investigate / refresh.
⚠️ CRITICAL PITFALL: bin edges from BASELINE only, then apply to actual. If you re-bin both independently, both look uniform → PSI ~ 0 regardless of drift.
Two applications: feature-level (has each input drifted?) and score-level (has output PD distribution drifted?).
Per-bin contributions reveal drift SHAPE, not just magnitude.
⭐ This is the answer to 'how do we trust customer behavior stays the same?' — PSI + adversarial classifier.

tech_hl.title: Hosmer-Lemeshow Calibration Test (INTERVIEW TRAP)
bullets: HL = Σ_j (O_j − E_j)² / [E_j(1 − E_j/n_j)] ~ χ²(g−2) — where j indexes groups of predicted probability.
H0: model is calibrated.
⚠️ TRAP: LARGE p-value = PASS (opposite of most tests). If p > 0.05, you FAIL to reject calibrated — good news.
⚠️ TRAP 2: can pass TRIVIALLY when the test has no power — e.g., middle bins sparse due to score saturation.
Always pair HL with reliability diagram AND check sample size per bin.
Reliability diagram: bin predicted PD, plot mean predicted vs mean actual. Diagonal = perfect calibration. Above = under-prediction (capital shortfall), below = over-prediction (capital waste).

tech_backtest.title: Binomial Backtest Traffic Light
bullets: Per rating grade: one-sided binomial test. H1: actual default rate > predicted PD.
p-value = P(X ≥ X_obs | X ~ Binomial(n, p_pred)).
⭐ ONE-SIDED because Basel cares about UNDER-prediction (capital shortfall). Over-prediction is conservative.
Traffic light: 🟢 p>0.10 continue | 🟡 0.05-0.10 enhanced monitoring | 🔴 p<0.05 TRIGGER RECALIBRATION.
A single red grade can trigger recalibration across the portfolio.
Common weakness: in low-default portfolios (LDP), test is underpowered — need alternative approaches.

tech_quasi_sep.title: Quasi-Separation (Model-Killing Finding)
bullets: Quasi-separation: a feature (or linear combination) perfectly predicts the outcome for some rows.
Symptom: coefficients drift toward ±∞, standard errors explode, p-values unreliable — the MLE doesn't converge sensibly.
If >20% of rows are perfectly predicted, the model output is not trustworthy.
FIXES: 1. Firth's penalized likelihood — adds a small bias to stabilize MLE.
2. Bayesian LR with weak prior — shrinks coefficients toward 0.
3. Exclude or re-bin the separating feature — may be leakage disguised.
4. INVESTIGATE — is the feature actually computed at a time AFTER the target is known? That's feature leakage.
Validator red flag: any LR output with coefficient |β| > 10 or warning about convergence deserves a closer look.

tech_parity_leak.title: Train/Test Parity ≠ No Leakage (4 Failure Modes)
bullets: A small train-test gap rules out OVERFITTING but NOT LEAKAGE.
Failure mode 1: Overfitting — train high, test much lower.
Failure mode 2: Feature leakage — train high, test equally high. Leakage contaminates both partitions.
Failure mode 3: Deterministic / synthetic data — train high, test equally high.
Failure mode 4: Train/serving skew — train fine, test fine, but model fails in production because live data differs.
⭐ When I see AUC > 0.95 on an independent test set, my next step isn't celebration — it's investigating feature derivation timestamps against the target's observation window.

tech_cal_vs_disc.title: Calibration vs Discrimination
bullets: DISCRIMINATION = can the model rank-order? Can it separate high risk from low? Measured by AUC / KS / Gini.
CALIBRATION = does a predicted 20% PD correspond to 20% actual default rate? Measured by reliability diagram + HL.
⭐ AUC can be 0.85 while calibration is terrible — e.g., model ranks correctly but systematically predicts 5% when true is 20%.
For MARKETING / scoring: discrimination dominates (rank matters).
For REGULATORY CAPITAL: CALIBRATION dominates (level matters) — because capital is a function of PD level, not rank.
⭐ Many DS practitioners don't know this distinction — making the point early impresses validators.

tech_reconciliation.title: Data Reconciliation (The Validator Reflex)
bullets: First 30 seconds of any validation: prove the dataset matches the authoritative source.
Checks: row counts vs business declaration; totals tie to General Ledger (to the dollar); date cutoffs (any leakage past as-of-date?); segment definitions match master; default flag per Basel 90-DPD?
Source-system lineage and refresh consistency.
⚠️ DISTINCT from data cleaning. Reconciliation = matching source. Cleaning = fixing problems.
Example: negative ages in data. Instead of silently applying abs(), FLAG as data integrity defect (likely ETL sign error), drop with documentation, escalate to data stewardship.
⭐ 'In validation, we don't silently correct — we document and escalate.'

tech_basel_pillars.title: Basel III — Pillars 1 / 2 / 3
bullets: PILLAR 1: Minimum capital requirements. Formula-driven, quantitative. Covers credit, market, operational risk.
PILLAR 2: Supervisory review. ICAAP (Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process), stress testing, economic capital. Regulator can require more capital than Pillar 1.
PILLAR 3: Market discipline. Public disclosure — banks publish risk metrics so the market can price risk correctly.
For this role: Pillars 1 and 2 matter most — AIRB parameters feed Pillar 1 capital, economic capital models support Pillar 2.
Basel III introduced after 2008 crisis. Added capital conservation buffer, countercyclical buffer, leverage ratio, liquidity ratios (LCR, NSFR).

tech_airb.title: AIRB vs F-IRB
bullets: IRB = Internal Ratings-Based approach (Basel). Banks use internal models to estimate risk parameters — subject to regulator approval.
F-IRB (Foundation): Bank estimates PD only. Regulator supplies LGD and EAD (standardized values).
A-IRB (Advanced): Bank estimates PD, LGD, and EAD all internally.
RBC uses A-IRB for Retail and most Wholesale — that's why EMRM validates all three parameters.
Alternative to IRB: Standardized Approach (uses external ratings, no internal models).
Validator role: independently validate each parameter's model, data, assumptions — critical for regulator to keep IRB approval.

tech_osfi_e23.title: OSFI E-23 / SR 11-7 — MRM Guidance
bullets: OSFI E-23 (Canada) — Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions guidance on Enterprise-Wide Model Risk Management.
SR 11-7 (US) — Federal Reserve equivalent. Both are industry benchmarks.
Both require: (1) independent validation by 2nd Line of Defense, (2) effective challenge of model developers, (3) documentation standards, (4) ongoing monitoring.
Key principle: models require full lifecycle governance — development, validation, approval, monitoring, retirement.
EMRM at RBC exists because of E-23. Every AIRB model must be independently validated before use and re-validated periodically.
If asked 'do you know E-23?': 'It's OSFI's MRM guidance requiring independent validation, effective challenge, documentation, and ongoing monitoring — I've been reading it to prepare.'

tech_reg_vs_econ.title: Regulatory vs Economic Capital
bullets: REGULATORY CAPITAL: prescribed by Basel / OSFI. Formula-driven minimum. Protects depositors and taxpayers.
ECONOMIC CAPITAL: bank's internal measure of capital needed to sustain unexpected losses at a target confidence level (typically ~99.95%, equivalent to AA rating).
Economic capital is usually STRICTER than regulatory — banks hold more than the minimum.
Economic capital uses internal portfolio models — CreditMetrics, CreditRisk+, KMV — typically with Monte Carlo simulation.
Both use PD/LGD/EAD as inputs, but aggregate differently.
Validator role: validate both regulatory parameters (AIRB) AND inputs to economic capital models.

tech_wholesale_retail.title: Wholesale vs Retail — Very Different Worlds
bullets: RETAIL: many small-balance customers (credit cards, mortgages, personal loans). Statistical models work because of sample size.
Retail approach: scorecard-based, LR on pools of similar customers. Behavioral features. Statistical backtesting robust.
WHOLESALE: few large-balance corporate / commercial customers. LOW DEFAULT PORTFOLIO (LDP) — maybe 0-5 defaults per year per segment.
Wholesale approach: Merton structural model (firm asset value vs debt threshold), expert judgment overlay, rating migration matrices.
Wholesale benchmarks: external ratings (Moody's, S&P).
Validation difference: Retail uses statistical significance; Wholesale uses qualitative review + external benchmarking because sample size too small for hypothesis tests.
⭐ Derek's 6.5 years were in Wholesale — he lived this.

tech_acct_vs_acq.title: Account Management vs Acquisition Models
bullets: ACQUISITION: scoring NEW APPLICANTS. Decisions: approve / decline / initial credit line.
Acquisition features: external — income, bureau score, employment, application variables.
ACCOUNT MANAGEMENT: scoring EXISTING BOOKED CUSTOMERS. Decisions: limit change, collections strategy, cross-sell, retention.
Account mgmt features: internal behavior — utilization, days past due trend, transaction patterns, payment behavior.
Account mgmt models generally more predictive because they use observed behavior (seasoning).
Observation window matters — features computed over a behavior window (e.g., 6 months), outcome measured in outcome window (e.g., next 12 months).
Validator focus: make sure observation and outcome windows don't overlap (leakage).

tech_ifrs9.title: IFRS 9 ECL (Expected Credit Loss)
bullets: IFRS 9 = accounting standard for credit loss provisioning. Replaced IAS 39 in 2018.
Forward-looking — requires banks to estimate EXPECTED credit losses, not just incurred losses.
Three stages: Stage 1 (12-month ECL), Stage 2 (lifetime ECL — significant increase in credit risk), Stage 3 (lifetime ECL — credit-impaired).
Uses point-in-time (PiT) PD — not the through-the-cycle (TTC) PD used for Basel capital.
Requires macroeconomic scenarios (base + adverse + favorable, weighted).
Validator angle: validate the PiT PD models, the stage-transition logic, and the scenario weighting.

tech_lgd.title: LGD Modeling (Beta / Tobit / Fractional)
bullets: Target: continuous 0-100%, typically BIMODAL at 0 and 1 (full recovery OR total loss).
BETA regression: natural for (0, 1) targets. Assumes beta distribution.
TOBIT: handles censoring at 0 or 1.
FRACTIONAL response: quasi-likelihood approach, robust to distribution assumptions.
TWO-STAGE: first classify 0-vs-positive LGD, then regress magnitude given positive.
Data types: WORKOUT LGD (actual recoveries tracked over time) vs MARKET LGD (prices of distressed debt).
Metrics: MAE, RMSE, Spearman correlation (NOT AUC — not a classification problem).
Calibration: compare mean predicted vs mean observed by segment.
Stability + backtesting: same PSI framework, different discrimination framework.

tech_ead.title: EAD + CCF (Credit Conversion Factor)
bullets: EAD = Exposure at Default — dollar amount at risk when the borrower defaults.
For TERM LOANS (mortgages, installment): EAD ≈ outstanding balance at default.
For REVOLVING (credit cards, lines of credit): EAD = drawn + CCF × (limit − drawn).
CCF (Credit Conversion Factor): empirical estimate of how much undrawn credit gets drawn as default approaches.
Empirically CCF > 0 — customers tend to DRAW DOWN their credit before defaulting.
Basel supplies CCF under F-IRB; banks estimate it internally under A-IRB.
Modeling: similar stack to LGD — Beta / fractional response on bounded [0, 1].
Validator angle: validate CCF estimation, segmentation, and the observation window (how far before default?).

tech_risk_cats.title: 5 Risk Categories
bullets: MARKET RISK — losses from market price movements. Measured by VaR, Expected Shortfall.
CREDIT RISK — losses from borrower default. Measured by EL = PD × LGD × EAD, and unexpected loss.
OPERATIONAL RISK — internal failures or external events. Measured by Risk Control Self-Assessment (RCSA), Key Risk Indicators (KRIs).
LIQUIDITY RISK — inability to meet short-term obligations. Measured by LCR, NSFR, cash position ratios.
CLIMATE RISK — recently added by OSFI / FRM. Physical risk (floods, fires) + transition risk (carbon regulation). Measured by scenario analysis, Climate VaR.
This role (EMRM) primarily focuses on CREDIT risk models — but cross-exposure to market risk (trading models) and operational risk (AML models) is common.

tech_var.title: VaR (Value at Risk)
bullets: VaR at confidence α over horizon h: the loss exceeded with probability (1−α) over h.
Example: 99% 1-day VaR of $10M means 1% chance of losing more than $10M tomorrow.
Three approaches: 1. Historical — use historical P&L distribution directly.
2. Parametric (variance-covariance) — assume normal, compute σ·z.
3. Monte Carlo — simulate P&L paths.
Weaknesses: doesn't describe TAIL beyond α. Replaced / supplemented by Expected Shortfall (ES) which averages losses beyond VaR.
Basel III moved from VaR to Expected Shortfall for market risk capital (Fundamental Review of the Trading Book).

tech_data_cleaning.title: Data Cleaning — 5-Step Framework
bullets: 1. Remove DUPLICATES or irrelevant observations.
2. Fix STRUCTURAL errors — typos, inconsistent naming, capitalization.
3. Filter unwanted OUTLIERS — but first prove they're errors, not legitimate extreme values.
4. Handle MISSING DATA — drop (if no meaning), impute (assumes data structure), or model-adjusted (handle nulls explicitly).
5. VALIDATE — confirm cleaned data is actually clean before modeling.
⚠️ Distinct from RECONCILIATION. Cleaning = fixing known problems. Reconciliation = proving data matches source.

tech_sup_unsup.title: Supervised vs Unsupervised ML
bullets: SUPERVISED: model trained on labeled data (input + correct output). Learns input→output mapping.
Examples: Linear / Logistic / Lasso / Ridge regression, Decision Trees, Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, Neural Networks.
In credit: PD models, LGD models, EAD models — all supervised.
UNSUPERVISED: model learns structure from unlabeled data.
Examples: K-means clustering, PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders, One-class SVM (for outliers).
In credit / AML: anomaly detection (Isolation Forest, One-class SVM), customer segmentation (K-means), feature construction (PCA).

tech_pca_tsne.title: PCA vs t-SNE
bullets: PCA (Principal Component Analysis): linear dimension reduction. Finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance.
PCA use: reduce variable count while retaining explanatory power. Components are interpretable as linear combinations.
t-SNE (t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding): NON-LINEAR. Visualizes high-dim data in 2D or 3D.
t-SNE preserves local neighborhoods (clusters). Bad for recovering global structure or distances between clusters.
t-SNE is for visualization, not feature engineering — don't feed t-SNE output into a downstream model.
In credit: PCA occasionally used to remediate multicollinearity (but destroys interpretability). t-SNE mostly for EDA.

tech_kmeans.title: K-means Clustering
bullets: Unsupervised clustering: partition n observations into K clusters where each obs belongs to the cluster with nearest mean.
Algorithm: 1. Initialize K centroids. 2. Assign each point to nearest centroid. 3. Update centroids to mean of assigned points. 4. Repeat until convergence.
Choosing K: elbow method (plot within-cluster SS vs K), silhouette score, domain knowledge.
Assumes: clusters are spherical and similar size. Poor on non-convex clusters (use DBSCAN or spectral).
Sensitive to initialization — use K-means++ for better starts.
In credit: customer segmentation, behavioral cohorts — feeding into differentiated scorecard models.

tech_svm.title: SVM + One-Class SVM
bullets: SVM = Support Vector Machine. Finds the hyperplane that BEST SEPARATES classes while MAXIMIZING MARGIN.
Kernel trick: project data into higher dimension for non-linear separation (RBF, polynomial).
Supervised classification — from sklearn.svm import SVC.
ONE-CLASS SVM: unsupervised. Learns the boundary of 'normal' points; anything outside = outlier.
Use case: anomaly / fraud / AML detection when you have mostly 'normal' data and few / no labeled anomalies.
from sklearn.svm import OneClassSVM.
In my Scotiabank pipeline: Isolation Forest for anomaly detection — similar philosophy but tree-based instead of kernel.

tech_nn.title: Neural Network + Backprop Basics
bullets: Architecture: input layer → hidden layers → output layer. Each connection has a weight; each neuron has a bias.
Forward pass: weighted sum of inputs + bias → activation function → next layer.
Activations: ReLU (hidden), sigmoid (binary output), softmax (multi-class output).
BACKPROPAGATION: when output is wrong, compute gradient of loss wrt each weight (chain rule), update weights via gradient descent.
Key hyperparameters: number of hidden layers, neurons per layer, learning rate, regularization (L1/L2/dropout).
⚠️ Neurons shouldn't be close to number of training samples → overfitting.
In credit risk: NNs used for fraud detection, account management scoring. For REGULATORY PD, interpretability usually wins and LR stays.

tech_autoencoder.title: Autoencoder
bullets: Neural network architecture trained to REPLICATE its input.
Two networks: ENCODER (input → latent representation) + DECODER (latent → reconstructed input).
Latent representation is a compressed / learned feature set.
Trained by minimizing reconstruction error (MSE between input and output).
Uses: dimension reduction (non-linear alternative to PCA), anomaly detection (high reconstruction error = anomaly), feature learning.
Variational Autoencoders (VAE): probabilistic version, can generate new samples.
In AML / fraud: train on normal transactions; anomalies have high reconstruction error.

tech_pytorch.title: PyTorch — Honest Answer
bullets: I've worked in PyTorch at the fundamentals level — tensors, autograd, basic training loops — but I haven't deployed deep-learning models in production.
My hands-on depth is in classical ML and statistical models, which I see as the right foundation for model validation in a regulated context where interpretability dominates.
For the multi-agent pipeline, the ML backbone I used was scikit-learn and Isolation Forest — LLMs were called via API rather than trained.
I'd build DL project depth on the job if I'm asked to validate internal DL models.
⚠️ Don't fake DL expertise — honest + methodology bridge is better.

tech_sas.title: SAS — Common Questions
bullets: IF vs WHERE in data step: WHERE is more efficient (runs before IF). WHERE can't be applied to newly created variables; IF can.
WHERE can only appear once; IF can appear multiple times. IF can use automatic variables (first., last.), WHERE cannot.
On MERGE: WHERE applies BEFORE merging; IF applies AFTER.
MERGE by key + keep matched: data D; merge A(in=a) B(in=b) C(in=c); by ACCT_NUM; if a and b and c; run;
STACK datasets: data D; set A B C; run;
REMOVE DUPLICATES: proc sort nodup; by key; run;
KEEP LATEST per key: proc sort data=... nodupkey; by key date DESCENDING; run;
Every 4th row: if mod(_n_, 4) = 0 then output;
PROC MEANS (numeric) vs PROC FREQ (categorical).
⚠️ I haven't done heavy SAS in production — but I can read it and know the syntax. I'd ramp quickly.

tech_sql.title: SQL — Common Questions
bullets: UNION removes duplicates; UNION ALL keeps them (faster).
JOINS: INNER (matching only), LEFT (all from left + matching right), RIGHT (mirror of left), FULL OUTER (all from both).
Last observation: SELECT * FROM t ORDER BY date DESC LIMIT 1; or with window function: SELECT * FROM (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY date DESC) rn FROM t) WHERE rn = 1;
Window functions: OVER(PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...). Common: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, SUM, AVG.
CTE: WITH x AS (SELECT ...) SELECT ... FROM x — readable multi-step queries.
GROUP BY rules: every non-aggregated column in SELECT must be in GROUP BY.
HAVING filters after aggregation; WHERE before.
NULL handling: IS NULL / IS NOT NULL (= NULL fails). COALESCE(x, default) for fallback.

tech_python.title: Python — Basics
bullets: LIST: ordered, mutable, indexed. [1,2,3]. L[0]=1, L[-1]=3 (negative indexing from end).
TUPLE: ordered, IMMUTABLE. (1,2,3).
DICT: key-value, mutable. {'a':1}. O(1) lookup.
SET: unordered, unique values, mutable. {1,2,3}. O(1) membership.
''.join(list_of_strings) → concatenates with delimiter. 'a,b,c'.split(',') → list.
List comp: [f(x) for x in list if cond].
Dict comp: {k:v for k,v in items}.
Generators: (x for x in ...) — lazy, memory-efficient.

tech_r.title: R — Basics
bullets: Basic data structures: vector, matrix, list, data.frame.
LAPPLY: apply function to each element of a list; returns list.
SAPPLY: apply function to each element of a list; returns vector (simplified).
APPLY: apply function over rows (MARGIN=1) or columns (MARGIN=2) of a matrix.
MAPPLY: multivariate — apply function with multiple argument vectors.
Subset: subset <- df[which(df$col == 'value'), ] or df[df$col == 'value', ].
dplyr: filter, select, mutate, group_by, summarize — modern / readable.

tech_brain_teasers.title: Brain Teasers (likely asked casually)
bullets: HANDSHAKE: N people in a party, each pair shakes once. Handshakes = N(N-1)/2. If 91 handshakes → N(N-1)=182 → N=14.
LILY PAD: doubles every day, full on day 60. Half on day 59 (one day before full).
DICE SUM 8 | FIRST=3: need second = 5. Prob = 1/6.
TWO GIRLS GIVEN ONE GIRL: sample space is {GG, GB, BG}. Only GG satisfies both girls. P = 1/3.
⚠️ These are common warm-ups. Don't overthink — say the framework clearly then compute.
